lens.included_activities: U.S. firms in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly provide printed circuit board loading, electronic assembly, module production, testing, and closely related engineering or supply-chain services to external OEM customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal assembly units, bare-board fabrication, printed circuit laminate production, finished-product OEM manufacturing, passive financing vehicles, shells, and firms without control-transferable operations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers outsource build-to-print or configured-to-order PCB assemblies and modules through purchase orders, forecasts, and product-lifecycle programs. Revenue is generally per assembly or production lot and can include engineering, test development, procurement, fulfillment, and aftermarket services; material costs are often contractually or commercially passed through.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for four-digit NAICS 334400 rather than 334418. Its 2023 mix includes 38.04% production, 22.72% architecture and engineering, 9.55% management, 6.70% business and financial operations, and 5.95% office and administrative support. The estimate assigns partial task exposure to engineering, quoting, planning, procurement, quality analysis, program management, and office work while excluding most physical assembly, setup, rework, test handling, and logistics. Because 334400 includes engineering-intensive semiconductor firms, it likely overstates engineering exposure for PCB assemblers; conversely, assembly firms have substantial planning and supply-chain information work.
a.rationale: AI can assist quotation, bill-of-material review, component substitution analysis, production planning, work-instruction drafting, quality analytics, procurement, documentation, and customer communication. Surface-mount operation, component placement, soldering, inspection handling, rework, test fixtures, and material movement remain physical or already automated.
a.change_evidence: A 334418-specific wage-weighted task inventory distinguishing current manual information work from existing SMT and inspection automation would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupational source is four-digit and includes semiconductor manufacturing.
Existing machine automation must not be counted again as AI opportunity.

rho.rationale: PCB assemblers already operate data-rich ERP, MRP, MES, automated inspection, and test workflows, supporting implementation in planning, quality, procurement, maintenance, and documentation. Realization is constrained by customer-controlled bills of material, fragmented legacy data, cybersecurity, regulated-market validation, component traceability, low-volume changeovers, and the need for human approval of substitutions and quality dispositions.
rho.change_evidence: Audited plant pilots reporting quotation hours, planner and buyer hours, schedule adherence, component shortages, false-call rates, scrap, rework, yield, downtime, and implementation costs would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No public five-year AI implementation study specific to 334418 was located.
Benefits may increase throughput or avoid hiring rather than reduce current headcount.

e.rationale: The six-digit definition closely matches outsourced repeat assembly, so many correctly sized independent firms should meet the operating lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive or affiliated plants, firms outside the EBITDA band despite firm-count bridging, severe customer concentration, weak quality systems, obsolete equipment, working-capital stress, or owner-dependent customer relationships.
e.change_evidence: A 334418 firm census linked to enterprise ownership, EBITDA, customer concentration, certifications, recurring program revenue, equipment condition, working capital, and management depth would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The frozen firm count is itself margin-bridged and may misclassify revenue bands.
Public EMS issuers illustrate the model but not the eligibility rate of LMM firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The estimate starts near that intention rate, then allows for an older manufacturing-owner population on the upside and discounts for plans that do not close, internal succession, strategic acquisitions, customer-transfer concerns, and distressed closures. The proxy likely overstates completed qualifying transfers even if it understates succession pressure.
s5.rationale: The large frozen target population and mature contract-manufacturing base create plausible transfer flow, but customer concentration, working capital, equipment needs, and owner-managed relationships can prevent a clean control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age microdata plus five years of 334418 closed transactions, broker listings, ESOPs, family transfers, strategic acquisitions, and closures would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses, not PCB assemblers.
Intentions are not completed control transfers.

q.rationale: Quality performance, approved-vendor status, product knowledge, test fixtures, supply-chain execution, regulated-market requirements, and switching disruption support some retention. However, EMS pricing is competitive, customers seek continuing cost reductions, materials are visible and often passed through, agreements can be purchase-order based, and OEMs can rebid or insource programs, limiting durable capture.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and purchase orders showing material pass-through, productivity clauses, annual price-downs, forecast commitments, program duration, renewal outcomes, and margin changes after process improvements would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Contract terms vary substantially by customer and regulated-market complexity.
Large public EMS firms may face stronger purchasing pressure than niche LMM assemblers.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 334400 from $140.2 billion in 2024 to $183.0 billion in 2034, a 2.7% annual rate; five years of that rate is approximately 1.14. The range is widened because 334418 assembly demand depends on outsourcing share, domestic versus offshore location, and end-market mix rather than component output alone. The broader proxy may overstate growth if semiconductor production dominates, or understate domestic assembly if OEM outsourcing and supply-chain regionalization increase.
d5.rationale: Nearly every complex electronic product requires loaded boards or electronic modules, and outsourcing offers OEMs flexibility, procurement scale, and lower fixed investment. Demand remains cyclical and exposed to offshoring, customer insourcing, short product lives, inventory corrections, component shortages, and price deflation.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 334418 shipments, assembly-unit volumes, U.S. outsourcing penetration, import content, bookings, customer forecasts, and program transfers onshore or offshore would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The BLS projection is four-digit and covers a ten-year horizon.
Revenue can rise or fall with component prices without a matching change in constant-quality assembly demand.

o.rationale: Loaded circuit boards require an accountable operator to procure and trace components, run placement and solder processes, inspect, test, rework, document, and deliver assemblies. AI and robotics can change staffing, while OEM insourcing can shift the operator, but software alone cannot supply the physical assembly.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widespread OEM self-service assembly, fully autonomous lights-out contract plants without accountable operational staff, or architectural elimination of PCB assemblies would materially change the estimate.
caveats: A shift from independent EMS to captive production reduces lens-operator demand without eliminating physical production.

narrative.executive_view: Printed circuit assembly is unusually close to the screen's recurring outsourced-service lens: independent EMS firms repeatedly manufacture customer-specific boards and modules. AI can improve information-heavy workflows, but competitive pricing and material pass-through restrain value capture while the physical operation remains indispensable.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest applications are quotation and BOM review, component-substitution research, planning, procurement, work instructions, automated-inspection triage, quality analytics, maintenance, traceability documentation, and customer updates. Placement, soldering, handling, rework, and test remain physical or already machine-automated.
narrative.value_capture: Approved-vendor status, fixtures, product knowledge, quality history, and regulated-market requirements create switching friction. OEM purchasing leverage, annual price-downs, competitive rebids, transparent component costs, and insourcing alternatives mean a substantial share of benefit is likely shared.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen firm count is large and the NAICS boundary fits external contract supply, supporting a meaningful eligible pool. Customer concentration, working-capital intensity, aging equipment, thin management, captive ownership, and founder-controlled relationships will still disqualify many firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Electronic products continue to require loaded boards, and outsourcing lets OEMs access flexible capacity and supply-chain expertise. Domestic demand can nevertheless diverge from end-product growth because work moves across borders and cycles with programs, inventory, component availability, and customer sourcing decisions.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are four-digit labor and demand proxies, material-price distortion in revenue, already-automated production work, customer and program concentration, offshore competition, working-capital swings, qualification transfer, and wide differences between commodity high-volume and regulated low-volume assemblers.
narrative.principal_driver: A large population of recurring outsourced assembly firms offers multiple repeatable opportunities to apply AI across planning, procurement, quality, and documentation.
narrative.principal_weakness: Powerful OEM customers, competitive rebids, material pass-through, and annual price pressure can prevent productivity gains from remaining with the operator.
